summary: My mother attended Mary Baldwin University, which was known even back then as a reputable school for women. She went there because they were looking for good schools, and Mary Baldwin had a strong reputation. It was quite far from home, but there weren't many options for women's education at the time, especially in the South. The education was more for learning and culture rather than for a career like teaching.

My mother also went to the Atlanta Female Institute, which might have some connection with Mary Baldwin University. I’m not exactly sure about the details, but she earned certificates from there. Like Mary Baldwin, it was one of the few good schools for women at the time, and my mother’s education was focused on culture and the arts rather than teaching. It was quite a distance from home, but they sought out quality education.

St. Mary's School in North Carolina was a well-established institution that many women attended for finishing school. My great aunt was there before the end of the Civil War, so it has a long history. It was one of the few options for women’s education in the South, focusing on arts and refinement. Many women went there to learn things like art, not necessarily to teach or earn a living.

My great aunt went on to what is now called Queens University of Charlotte, though it wasn’t known by that name back then. She pursued further education there after attending St. Mary's. Like my mother, she didn’t teach but was very involved in cultural and artistic pursuits. Her education was part of a family tradition valuing learning for its own sake.

I noticed that some of my mother’s paintings resembled styles I’ve seen at the Smithsonian Institution in Washington. She did oil paintings, charcoal drawings, and painted on china, often depicting homes of writers, flowers, and birds. The Smithsonian’s collection helped me recognize the artistic style that was popular in her time. It’s a connection that shows how her work fit into a broader artistic tradition.

I grew up in Fayetteville, North Carolina, a historic town of about 8,000 people during my childhood. It was a place rich in history, especially with the Market House at the center of town and the memory of La Fayette’s visit after the American Revolution. My great aunt was very active in the community there, leading efforts to preserve the Market House. Fayetteville shaped much of my early life and sense of civic duty.

entity: Gilbert du Motier, Marquis de Lafayette
summary: Gilbert du Motier, Marquis de Lafayette, visited Fayetteville on his famous tour of America after the Revolution. The town honored him warmly, and his visit became a cherished part of local history. This connection inspired my great aunt and others to preserve the Market House where he was entertained. Lafayette’s legacy was a point of pride for our community and influenced our civic activities.

The Market House in Fayetteville was a central and historic building where meat was sold and, according to some, slaves were once sold, though that is disputed. It blocked the intersection of four streets and was considered an obstruction by some newcomers, but the older residents valued its history. My great aunt was president of a civic club that raised money to restore and preserve the Market House when there was talk of tearing it down. I remember selling movie tickets as a child to help raise funds for its upkeep.

One of my teachers at the private school I attended was Miss Alice Mallett, who later married Major Hale, the American minister or ambassador to Cuba. This connection was notable because it linked our small-town school to international affairs. Though I didn’t have a direct relationship with Cuba, this personal tie through my teacher was a memorable part of my early education experience.
